title: Tidings of Comfort and Joy
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: 11-17-24 -PM- Tidings of Comfort and Joy - Psalm 30_4-5 - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Joy Is Not Happiness
subpoints: Prompted by a discussion in Nick Anderson's book group on Gavin Ortlund's Humility: The Joy of Self-Forgetfulness — true humility always leads to joy
Joy does not mean happiness or pleasant circumstances; the life of a believer is marked by understanding the goodness of God
Job questioned God's goodness in affliction, yet when God answered from the whirlwind Job received comfort and joy in knowing who God is and who we are
The great cloud of witnesses throughout church history — their lives are marked by comfort and joy rooted in God's sovereignty and goodness

outline.title: 1. We Are Called to Sing Praise and Give Thanks (v.4)
subpoints: Verse 4: Sing praise to the Lord, you saints of his, and give thanks at the remembrance of his holy name
The psalms repeatedly exhort and command the people of God to sing praise and give thanks — we have done so this Lord's Day in the great assembly
Psalm 46: God is our refuge and strength; there is a river whose streams make glad the city of God — the city shall not be moved
Isaiah 55:1 and John 7:37-38 — the living water that makes glad the city is Christ himself, the fountain filled with blood drawn from Emmanuel's veins
We are to remember: we who were an adulterous harlot like Gomer have received the promise of Hosea 2 — 'I will betroth you to me forever... you are my people'

outline.title: 2. It Is Here That We Find True Tidings of Comfort and Joy (v.5)
subpoints: Verse 5: For his anger is but for a moment, his favor is for life; weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning

In seasons of death, weeping, loss, and struggle — the death of a child, a failing parent, cancer, suicide, a broken marriage, a child walking away from faith — we may feel that God's anger is for a lifetime and the night will never end
The Psalms give voice to this: Psalm 10, Psalm 44, Psalm 88 — the latter closes with no declaration of praise, only darkness and silence

The comfort is found in the first truth of v.5: his anger is for a moment — it was poured out fully on his only begotten Son; the chastisement of our peace was upon him, and by his wounds we are healed

The second truth: weeping may endure for a night but joy comes in the morning — because Christ bore the anger we deserved, pleads for us at the Father's right hand, and will return to gather his bride
Isaiah 40:1-2, 9 — Comfort, yes, comfort my people... O Zion, behold your God; our Lord God knows your wanderings, has put your tears in his bottle, and our beloved bridegroom will come again

summary: Mr. Isaiah Couch delivers an afternoon sermon on Psalm 30:4-5 under the title "Tidings of Comfort and Joy" — not a Christmas message, but a reflection on the nature of true joy arising from a book group discussion on Gavin Ortlund's claim that genuine humility always leads to joy. The sermon establishes that biblical joy is not happiness or freedom from trial: Job, David, and the great cloud of witnesses all demonstrate lives marked by comfort and joy rooted in knowing who God is and who we are. The first point draws on verse 4 — the command to sing praise and give thanks — showing that the city of God is made glad by the living waters of Christ himself, and that God's people, once adulterous harlots like Gomer, have received the covenant promise: "you are my people." The second point enters honestly into the experience of seasons of loss and grief — the death of a child in the womb, a spouse's failing health, a friend's suicide, a marriage falling apart — acknowledging the raw lament voices of Psalms 10, 44, and 88. The comfort of verse 5 is found in this: God's anger was poured out in full on his Son at the cross; his favor toward his people is for a lifetime; and the weeping night will end in a morning of joy when Christ returns to gather his bride. The sermon closes with Isaiah 40: Comfort my people — behold your God.
transcript:
## Introduction: True Joy Is Not Happiness

Good afternoon, everyone, and a happy Lord's Day once again to you all. It has certainly been a blessed Lord's Day so far. My hope and prayer this afternoon is that we are able to continue to be blessed by our kind and merciful God by looking at a small portion of Scripture — Psalm 30, verses 4 and 5.

The title and theme has its genesis from a discussion we had in Nick Anderson's group study last month. As we all know, we have been going through the book *Humility: The Joy of Self-Forgetfulness* by Gavin Ortlund. In chapter two, he said that true humility always leads to joy — and that this is the test to see if our humility is genuine or merely false. While we can pretend to be joyful, pretend to have peace, pretend to have comfort, our hearts may be found joyless, unpeaceful, and comfortless.

Our book group spent time discussing whether this statement was true. And as the discussion unfolded, we saw that joy does not always mean happiness. It does not mean pleasant circumstances. It does not mean we will never experience trials. But the life of a believer is marked by understanding the goodness of God. Their life will be marked by comfort and joy.

We see Job — certainly not always happy. There were times when he brought God's sovereignty into question. But when God answers Job from the whirlwind and gives him all the ways he has been good and sovereign, Job is left with the recognition of who God is — and there is joy and comfort in knowing who we are and who God is.

King David — a liar, a thief, an adulterer, a murderer, a father who lost his son, feeling betrayed and being betrayed — certainly not always happy. But David again and again speaks of comfort and joy. He declares in Psalm 4:7: "You have put more joy in my heart than they have when their grain and wine abound."

The great cloud of witnesses throughout church history — their lives are marked over and over again with hearts that understand the joy and comfort in knowing who God is and who we are.

## Reading Psalm 30

My passage this afternoon is Psalm 30. Let us begin by reading the whole psalm, and then we will zoom in on two verses.

Psalm 30 verse 1: *I will extol you, O Lord, for you have lifted me up and have not let my foes rejoice over me. O Lord my God, I cried out to you and you healed me. O Lord, you brought my soul up from the grave. You have kept me alive that I should not go down to the pit. Sing praises to the Lord, you saints of his, and give thanks at the remembrance of his holy name. For his anger is but for a moment, his favor is for life. Weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning. Now in my prosperity I said, I shall never be moved. Lord, by your favor you have made my mountain stand strong. You hid your face and I was troubled. I cried out to you, O Lord, and to the Lord I made supplication. What profit is there in my blood when I go down to the pit? Will the dust praise you? Will it declare your truth? Hear, O Lord, and have mercy on me. Lord, be my helper. You have turned for me my mourning into dancing. You have put off my sackcloth and clothed me with gladness. To the end that my glory may sing praises to you and not be silent. O Lord my God, I will give thanks to you forever.*

Robert Godfrey says in *Learning to Love the Psalms*: "We must never rush through the Psalms, but learn to pause, ponder, and meditate on them, so that their poetic beauty and profound truths will penetrate deeply into our hearts." I would add: we will find more and more comfort and joy as we turn to God's word.

## 1. We Are Called to Sing Praise and Give Thanks (v.4)

Verse 4: *Sing praise to the Lord, you saints of his, and give thanks at the remembrance of his holy name.*

The Psalms again and again exhort and command the people of God to sing praise to the Lord and give thanks. Psalm 35:18 says, "I will give thanks in the great assembly. I will praise you among many people." We have done that this Lord's Day.

Psalm 48:1-3: *Great is the Lord and greatly to be praised in the city of our God... the joy of the whole earth is Mount Zion... God is in her palaces, he is known as her refuge.* The people of God are said to be the ones who know God is their refuge.

Psalm 46: *God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. Therefore we will not fear even though the earth be removed.* There is a river whose streams shall make glad the city of God. God is in the midst of her; she shall not be moved.

Isaiah 55:1: *Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters.* These waters flow with living water that bring spiritual life to those who drink by faith. Jesus said in John 7:37-38: *If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink. He who believes in me, as the Scripture has said, out of his heart will flow rivers of living water.*

This is the fountain filled with blood drawn from Emmanuel's veins. And this is what it means to sing praise and give thanks at the remembrance of his holy name — to remember that our kind and merciful God has become our God, and we have become his bride, his people.

1 Peter 2:9-10: *But you are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, his own special people, that you may proclaim the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light — who once were not a people but now are the people of God.*

We are to remember that we who were once an adulterous and idolatrous harlot like Gomer — enemies of God, children of wrath, dead in sins and trespasses — have through the Lord Jesus Christ received the promise of Hosea 2:19-23: *I will betroth you to me forever... I will say to those who were not my people, "You are my people," and they shall say, "You are my God."*

We sing praise and give thanks because we, an undeserving people, have received mercy. The reality of this glorious truth declares tidings of comfort and joy to the people of God.

## 2. It Is Here That We Find True Tidings of Comfort and Joy (v.5)

Verse 5: *For his anger is but for a moment, his favor is for life. Weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning.*

There are seasons and times of laughing, embracing, loving, and dancing. In those times, the praise of God comes easily. But when a child dies in the womb, or a beloved parent or spouse's health is failing, or a brother or sister finds out they have cancer, or a dear friend commits suicide, or a marriage is falling apart, or a child walks away from the faith — when we are in a time and season of death, weeping, loss, silence, and struggle — we may feel the opposite of verse 5. We may feel that God's anger is for a lifetime, and that our weeping has endured what seems to be an endless night where the morning light will never come.

We see in the Psalms moments where the Psalmist expresses his struggle, his questions, his outrage. Psalm 10:1: *Why do you stand afar off, O Lord? Why do you hide in times of trouble?* Psalm 44: *Arise, do not cast us off forever. Why do you hide your face and forget our affliction?* Psalm 88, one of the psalms I always told people always ended in praise — until someone asked me about verse 14: *Lord, why do you cast off my soul? Why do you hide your face from me? I have been afflicted and ready to die from my youth.* It ends in darkness, with loved ones and friends put far away and acquaintances into darkness.

So we may ask: where are the tidings of comfort and joy? Where is the joy that comes in the morning?

It is found in verse 5 itself. First: *his anger is but for a moment.* There is great comfort and joy for the believer who understands that the one living and true God — infinite, immutable, most just, hating all sin, who will by no means clear the guilty — this God poured out upon his only begotten Son the anger, wrath, judgment, and punishment for sin that we deserved. It was in his hating of sin that it pleased the Father to crush him, to lay upon him the chastisement and iniquity of us all, so that by his wounds we are healed.

Second: *weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning.* There is comfort in knowing that Christ has regarded our helpless estate. He bore the anger we deserved. He became sin who knew no sin. He has clothed us in his righteousness. He pleads for us at the right hand of the Father. And he will return to gather his bride to himself.

So when sorrows like sea-billows roll, or Satan should buffet and trials should come — it is well with my soul. As Spurgeon said: "I have learned to kiss the waves that throw me against the rock of ages."

Isaiah 40:1-2 and 9: *Comfort, yes, comfort my people, says your God. Speak comfort to Jerusalem. O Zion, you who bring good tidings... say to the cities of Judah, "Behold your God."*

So brothers and sisters — those looking for comfort and joy and peace in this life — behold your God. Hear the tidings of comfort and joy. Our Lord God knows your wanderings. He has put your tears into his bottle. They are counted. He has searched you and knows you. He has called us his people and we call him our God. He has redeemed us from the curse of the law. And our beloved bridegroom will come again to bring us to glory forevermore.

*Sing praise to the Lord, you saints of his, and give thanks at the remembrance of his holy name. For his anger is but for a moment, his favor is for life. Weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning.*
